Title: Soeder baut aus und um
Article_Text: Soeder baut aus und um 24.03.2026 Der Zürcher Naturpflegehersteller Soeder steigerte den Umsatz vergangenes Jahr um 25 Prozent und schloss im September eine Finanzierungsrunde ab. Gleichzeitig wurde die Führung neu geordnet. Mit Karoline Huber hat eine erfahrene Führungspersönlichkeit in der internationalen Luxusgüterbranche die Unternehmensleitung übernommen. Vor einem Jahr haben die Gründer Hanna Olzon Akerström und Johan Olzon Akerström den geplanten Übergang von einer Gründer-geführten Organisation zu einer skalierbar strukturierten Führungs- und Governance-Struktur eingeleitet und ziehen sich nun schrittweise aus dem operativen Geschäft zurück. Hanna Olzon Akerström verliess das operative Geschäft 2025; Johan Olzon Akerström wird seine operative Rolle bis Ende Juni 2026 abgeben. Beide bleiben dem Unternehmen eng verbunden. Gleichzeitig konnte Soeder 2025 mit Karoline Huber eine erfahrene Führungspersönlichkeit als CEO holen und unter ihr den eingeschlagenen Wachstumskurs akzentuieren: 2025 steigerte das Unternehmen sein operatives Ergebnis um 30 Prozent. „Wir setzen uns weiterhin hohe Ziele in Sachen Wachstum. Es wird aber nicht Wachstum um jeden Preis, Langfristigkeit ist unsere Devise“, so Karoline Huber, die über umfangreiche Erfahrung in der Luxusgüterbranche verfügt und zuvor leitende Positionen bei IWC Schaffhausen, Jaeger-LeCoultre, Richemont, Santoni und Swarovski innehatte. Die Eigentümerbasis konnte mit einer erfolgreichen Finanzierungsrunde im September 2025 weiter gestärkt werden. Gleichzeitig wurde das Produktportfolio gezielt verfeinert und erweitert: Body Wash, Hand Cream und ein Raumspray ergänzen nun das Sortiment, während versandfähige Nachfülllösungen aus recycelbarem Material einen weiteren Schritt in Richtung Zirkularität darstellen. «Dass wir auf dem richtigen Weg sind», so Karoline Huber, «haben wir bereits im vergangenen Jahr gespürt. Etliche unserer Neulancierungen, darunter die Hand Cream, Body Wash oder Nachfüll-Pouches sind bereits Bestseller», erklärt sie weiter. (Press release / SK) Bild: Karoline Huber / Soeder 0 Comments FINANCING SUCCESS More news about Soeder Related News Soeder 09.08.2024 11:37 Soeder sichert sich Unterstützung von Georges Kern Related tags

Title: Cohaga sammelt Millionenbetrag und lanciert Tool für Präsenz in KI-Antworten
Article_Text: Cohaga sammelt Millionenbetrag und lanciert Tool für Präsenz in KI-Antworten 02.04.2026 Die St. Galler cohaga AG betreibt die führende Schweizer Direktmarketing Plattform LeadHub. Das 2020 gestartete Unternehmen ist bootstrapped auf 30 Personen gewachsenen. Nun wurde erstmal eine Finanzierungsrunde durchgeführt. Gleichzeitig wurde ein neues Tool lanciert, das Unternehmen so positioniert, dass sie von KI nicht nur erkannt, sondern aktiv empfohlen und zitiert werden. Mitte März hat die cohaga AG eine Kapitalerhöhung durchgeführt. Zwei erfahrene Unternehmer und Business Angels aus dem Tech- und SaaS-Bereich beteiligen sich jetzt mit einem siebenstelligen Betrag am Unternehmen. «Diese Kapitalerhöhung ist für uns weit mehr als eine reine Finanzierung – sie ist ein klares Signal für das Vertrauen in unsere Vision und den bisherigen Weg. Wir haben bewusst lange unabhängig agiert und schaffen jetzt die Voraussetzungen, um unsere Entwicklung gezielt beschleunigen zu können.» sagt Isabel Bischof, Mitgründerin und COO der cohaga AG. Das zusätzliche Kapital ermöglichte es dem Team von COHAGA, in den vergangenen Monaten intensiv an einem neuen Produkt zu arbeiten, welches das Potential hat, den Marketing-Bereich nachhaltig zu verändern. «Während sich der cohaga LeadHub um Outbound-Leads kümmert, wird das neue Produkt mit dem Namen ranQ unseren Kunden planbar Inbound-Leads einbringen», ist Fabio Mätzler, Mitgründer und CEO überzeugt. Das neue Produkt hilft Unternehmen vom grundlegenden Wandel der Internetsuche von klassischen Suchmaschinen hin zu direkt generierten Antworten durch Systeme wie ChatGPT zu profitieren. Im Zentrum steht nicht mehr die Platzierung in Suchresultaten, sondern die direkte Integration in Antworten – dort, wo Nutzer heute Entscheidungen treffen. ranQ basiert auf dem Ansatz der sogenannten Generative Engine Optimization (GEO), einer Weiterentwicklung der klassischen Suchmaschinenoptimierung für KI-Systeme. Ziel ist es, Unternehmen so zu positionieren, dass sie von KI nicht nur erkannt, sondern aktiv empfohlen und zitiert werden. Damit greift ranQ in einen Markt ein, der sich aktuell grundlegend neu formiert: Sichtbarkeit entsteht nicht mehr über Rankings, sondern über Relevanz innerhalb von KI-Antworten. Im neuen Wettbewerbsumfeld gewinnt nicht mehr die lauteste, sondern die relevanteste Information. Wer es schafft, von KI-Systemen als vertrauenswürdige Quelle eingeordnet zu werden, sichert sich einen nachhaltigen Vorteil. Noch befindet sich dieser Markt in einer frühen Phase, doch die Dynamik ist hoch und die Entwicklung nicht mehr umkehrbar. «Die zunehmende Nutzung von KI verändert Kaufentscheidungen nachhaltig. Immer mehr Nutzer verlassen sich auf KI-generierte Empfehlungen, die gezielter und vertrauenswürdiger wahrgenommen werden. Unternehmen, die frühzeitig in dieser neuen Form der Sichtbarkeit investieren, sichern sich entscheidende Wettbewerbsvorteile», sagt Fabio Mätzler, CEO der cohaga AG. (Press release / SK) 0 Comments FINANCING LAUNCH More news about cohaga AG Company profiles on startup.ch cohaga AG Related News cohaga AG 11.01.2024 17:00 Adressdaten-Spezialist COHAGA auf Wachstumskurs Related tags

Title: Solar Monkey and Eturnity merge and attract fresh capital
Article_Text: Solar Monkey and Eturnity merge and attract fresh capital 27.03.2026 Eturnity and Solar Monkey merge to form the European leader in installer software. The new company is backed with fresh capital by a group of European investors. Together, Solar Monkey, founded in 2015 in the Netherlands, and Swiss-based Eturnity deliver software to more than 2,000 installers across 8+ European countries. Their software provides the backbone for the full installer process, from design and sales to installation and permitting. This enables installers to tackle the complexities of today’s energy market and improve their cost competitiveness. The merger enables the combination of Solar Monkey’s software for intuitive design and sales with Eturnity’s strength in end-to-end workflows and complex project engineering. The full range of energy installations and related products will be supported. With a footprint across 8 European countries, the new entity can provide superior economies of scale and emerges as the leading installer software in Europe. New investment The new company is backed with fresh capital by investors including Klima (Spain), Junction (Belgium), Eneco Ventures (Netherlands) and Helen (Finland). As part of a capital increase, Eturnity AG has acquired shares in Solar Monkey B.V. and issued new registered shares in return. Furthermore, Solar Monkey founder and CEO Jan Pieter Versluijs and Anusha Nirmalananthan, a non-executive director at Solar Monkey, have joined the Board of Directors of Eturnity AG. Both brands and products continue to operate in the foreseeable future. At the same time, a stronger and more comprehensive range of software products will be developed. The market is rapidly expanding beyond solar panels toward complete home electrification systems. Installers are moving from single-product solar projects to integrated energy systems including products like batteries and heat pumps. This drastically increases the complexity of the installers’ jobs. At the same time, the pressure on labour costs (which accounts to 60% of the total cost) is mounting. “Basically, all installers need to grasp this increasing complexity”, says Matthias Wiget, CEO of Eturnity. “Installers can no longer compete without a strong software backbone to streamline their increasingly complicated work. From lead to installation, every step in the workflow needs to be digitised. That’s where the biggest gains are.” The founders of both companies have known each other for years through the European solar industry. “We always shared respect for what the other team was building,” says Jan Pieter Versluijs, founder of Solar Monkey. “By joining forces, we combine deep market knowledge and complementary strengths across markets and products. That allows us to innovate faster and build even better software for installers across Europe.” (Press release / SK) 0 Comments FINANCING More news about Eturnity AG Company profiles on startup.ch Eturnity AG Highlights Eturnity AG Innosuisse Certificate Winner Venture Kick Related News Eturnity AG 07.06.2023 10:00 Eturnity: Acht Millionen Franken für weiteres Wachstum 15.05.2023 16:30 Swissnex San Francisco reveals first cohorts for its sector-based bootcamps Related tags

Title: Delta Labs raises EUR 4.4 million for AI customer simulation platform
Article_Text: Delta Labs raises EUR 4.4 million for AI customer simulation platform 02.04.2026 Delta Labs has raised EUR 4.4 million in a seed round led by Cusp Capital and Auxxo Female Catalyst Fund. The company develops AI-powered customer simulation tools that help businesses anticipate how customers will respond to decisions before they are made. Most companies rely on surveys, focus groups and interviews to understand their customers. However, customers may be unreliable narrators of their own preference, which means the research that informs product, pricing and messaging decisions is distorted. Therefore, applying AI to accelerate those same inputs, as many tools now do, does not fix the underlying problem. Rather than generating responses from AI personas trained on generic internet data, Delta Lab’s first AI-tool Elaiia, which launched six months ago, builds a probabilistic model of a company's actual customer distribution, enriched with their own first-party signals. This allows teams to simulate how customers are likely to respond across product, pricing, messaging and strategy decisions ahead of time. Delta Labs was founded in 2022 by Anne Scherer, a former University of Zurich professor, and Cindy Candrian, who completed her PhD there. Their work draws on combined expertise in market research, quantitative modelling and human-technology interaction. The company had been bootstrapped until this funding round. The EUR 4.4 million seed round was backed by Cusp Capital and the Auxxo Female Catalyst Fund. Cusp Capital is an Essen, Germany-based venture capital firm focused on early-stage European software and technology companies, while the Auxxo Female Catalyst Fund is a Berlin-based early-stage fund established in 2019 that co-invests in European startups with at least one female founder holding a minimum of 20% of the founding shares. (AJ) Photo: Delta Labs Team. Photo courtesy of Delta Labs. 0 Comments FINANCING More news about Delta Labs AG Related tags

Title: BIND 2026 Crowns Four Swiss Health Gems
Article_Text: BIND 2026 Crowns Four Swiss Health Gems 02.04.2026 Four Swiss life sciences startups were honored at BIND - BioInnovation Day 2026, an event brimming with discoveries and insights. Dexterous Endoscope won the award for Best Startup, while PreciMune received the award for Best University Project. Impli AG and MoleSense secured support from H4 and CSEM. BioAlps proudly celebrated the most promising life sciences innovations emerging from Western Switzerland at the BIND – BioInnovation Day 2026, held at CHUV and attended by 340 participants from academia, healthcare, industry, investment and innovation support. Now firmly established as one of the region’s flagship innovation events, BIND brings together the Swiss Health Valley ecosystem to showcase breakthrough academic projects and fast-rising startups shaping the future of healthcare. In addition to more than 30 life sciences startup pitches, the day featured a round table, an insightful Investor Session and an inspiring keynote address by Vincent Delattre, CEO of Onward. This year’s edition recognized four standout winners, each addressing major unmet needs in medicine and health technologies: Dexterous Endoscopes Best Startup Sponsored by Lenz & Staehelin (CHF 5,000) Dexterous Endoscopes is an EPFL spin-off. The project stood out for its next-generation variable-stiffness endoscope, designed to combine the navigational advantages of flexible instruments with the precision and force of rigid tools. The technology is particularly relevant for procedures in ENT, urinary and gastrointestinal pathways — areas where access remains a major clinical challenge. The company has also recently secured significant support, including CHF 150,000 through Venture Kick and CHF 250,000 from the Canton of Vaud to accelerate product development and growth. PreciMune – Best Academic Project Award Sponsored by KT UNIL - CHUV and UNITEC (CHF 5,000) PreciMune, developed at UNIL-CHUV with support from the Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research, is advancing a proprietary immune profiling platform capable of delivering highly sensitive and comprehensive immune repertoire analysis. The project aims to support more precise patient selection, earlier treatment monitoring, and more effective development of next-generation therapies. In early 2026, PreciMune also received a FIT-InnoTREK grant. Impli AG – H4 Award Based at Biopôle, Impli AG is developing what could become the first real-time hormone monitoring device for women’s health. By enabling continuous hormone tracking, the company aims to transform fertility care, IVF monitoring, and broader hormone-related health management. The startup recently gained international visibility through a strategic partnership with Bayer, following its win in a Bayer-sponsored innovation challenge. MoleSense – CSEM Award Finally, MoleSense, another breakthrough project originating from EPFL, is developing a needle-free wearable patch for continuous and non-invasive monitoring of reproductive health biomarkers, with an initial focus on high-risk pregnancies. By replacing repeated blood draws and enabling real-time monitoring, the technology has the potential to ease the burden on both patients and clinicians. The company recently received CHF 100,000 from the Fondation pour l’Innovation Technologique (FIT) and is currently preparing for its next stage of development and fundraising. MoleSense will receive strategic and technological support from CSEM. Showcasing the best academic research and innovation BIND – BioInnovation Day was originally launched by UNITEC, the technology transfer office of the University of Geneva (UNIGE) and the Geneva University Hospitals (HUG), together with KT UNIL-CHUV (formerly PACTT), the technology transfer office of the University of Lausanne (UNIL) and the Lausanne University Hospital (CHUV). The event was created to showcase the best academic life sciences research and innovation emerging from Western Switzerland. Since 2025, BioAlps – Western Switzerland Life Sciences Cluster – has taken over the organization of BIND, further strengthening its role as a platform connecting research, entrepreneurship, healthcare and industry across the Swiss Health Valley. Title: Introducing our principal and key partners
Article_Text: Introducing our principal and key partners 21.10.2020 Startupticker.ch can provide free high-quality content thanks to our supporters. Find out more about our principal Innosuisse and key partners Gebert Rüf Stiftung, Deep Tech Nation Switzerland Foundation and UBS. Principal: Innosuisse - Swiss Innovation Agency Innosuisse’s role is to promote science-based innovation in the interests of industry and society in Switzerland. It especially promotes the partnership between academia and the market with national and international innovation projects, networking, training and coaching, laying the groundwork for successful Swiss start-ups, products and services. The Innosuisse Guide helps you find the right support offer in just a few steps (www.innosuisse.guide). Key Partner: Gebert Rüf Stiftung The Gebert Rüf Stiftung was founded by entrepreneur Heinrich Gebert as a science and innovation foundation with the aim of "strengthening Switzerland as a top business location and a place to live". The foundation’s role is to take risks and provide funding in order to boost its mission statement – ‘Making science effective’. It positions itself in the ‘valley of death’, where for many innovative projects the grant-making chain breaks down, research funds are no longer available and no venture capital is in sight. The funding policy applies in particular to projects of up-and-coming entrepreneurial talents who want to pursue their own paths. www.grstiftung.ch/en Key Partner: Deep Tech Nation Switzerland Foundation Deep Tech Nation Switzerland Foundation acts as a catalyst for the Swiss startup ecosystem, focusing on the deep tech sector. Our mission is to empower startups and scale-ups to become global players in their market by mobilizing CHF 50 billion in venture capital and creating 100,000 jobs by 2033. We aim to strengthen Switzerland's long-term innovation and global competitiveness through these efforts. In an interview with Swisspreneur, CEO Joanne Sieber explains the plans, the fields of activity and first steps of the foundation in depth. To learn more about this economics initiative and order the book that started it all, go to: https://deeptechnation.ch/ Key Partner: UBS UBS is the premier Swiss bank for start-ups, scale-ups and mature companies. We help entrepreneurs strive and realize their private and business goals with tailor-made offerings. Let’s write your success story together! Check out UBS Start Business for start-ups and UBS Growth Advisory for growth-stage scale-ups or simply contact Lukas Reinhardt for a personal chat. Find out more about Lukas Reinhardt, the UBS programs for startups and investments in general in our interview or this podcast from Swisspreneur. 0 Comments KEY PARTNER Principal Investments 2026 The Swiss Venture Capital Report 2026 offers key insights into the latest trends shaping the investment landscape. Title: Younger investment firms turn to diversity
Article_Text: Younger investment firms turn to diversity 01.04.2026 Only 7% of venture funding goes to women-led startups—a gap the FE+MALE Think Tank aimed to address through its first Biennal & Award event, which brought together over 100 interested stakeholders. Investors and support organisations highlighted the stronger performance of diverse teams at the event. The young impact investment firm iGravity was among three organizations recognized at the award ceremony for its efforts to direct capital toward women-led businesses. The Beat the Funding Bias Initiative (BFBI), led by FE+MALE Think Tank, held its first Biennial & Award event in Zürich, bringing together 115 participants from the start-up and innovation ecosystem to address unconscious bias in entrepreneurship funding. Only 7% of startup funding goes to women-led ventures in Switzerland, a gap that the BFBI is working to close. Through panels, keynotes and masterclasses at its inaugural Biennial, the initiative made the case that founding team diversity is not only equitable, but also financially sound. “Women founders bring to the table very high resilience and a high quality of innovation. Most of the time women develop solutions to a real problem—like in healthcare, childcare and climate issues—where there is a high impact to society. Then, business-wise, this has a real chance to succeed,” says Désirée Dosch, managing partner at the investment firm Alvicus. Alvicus was founded in 2020 specifically to support female-led ventures, and the firm recently led a financing round for Swiss start-up rayne care. MassChallenge Switzerland, a non-profit, zero-equity accelerator based in Renens, has tracked founder diversity for five years. "Diverse teams are stronger teams," said Hendrik Dik of MassChallenge Switzerland. "Diversity in a leadership team allows people to see problems from different perspectives, reduces bias in decision making, and leads to better outcomes across the board. It is more than fair, it is smart business." iGravity wins Catalyst Capital award Of more than 100 nominations, three organizations received awards at the ceremony. With iGravity, an impact investment management and advisory firm founded in 2017, a young company has also made it onto the list of winners. iGravity also focuses on female-led teams, but in the Global South. The company won the Catalyst Capital and Community Award for its mobilization of capital, resources and partnerships toward community impact. The firm manages between USD 50 and 100 million in assets. In February 2026 alone, more than 1'000 micro, small and medium enterprises received financial support through iGravity’s Investment Solutions, with 81% of those ventures being women-owned. "At iGravity, we see the need, we see the possibility and we go for it. We believe that change is a step-by-step process and we can assure you that it works," said Heidi Foppa, board member of iGravity, in her acceptance speech. Established organizations IKEA and the International Trade Center received the Modern Leadership Award and the Decision Making and Value Chain Award, respectively. Patricia M. Montesinos, founder and president of the FE+MALE Think Tank, said the event marked a transition from convening to action. "If even a fraction of these actors becomes active investors in and supporters of women-led startups—and help scale this across Switzerland—the impact becomes systemic," she said. (AJ) Photo: Participants of the Biennal & Award 2026 event organized by FE+MALE Think Tank. Photo courtesy of FE+MALE Think Tank. Title: KI-Tool erleichtert Ausdauersport-Coaches weltweit die Arbeit
Article_Text: KI-Tool erleichtert Ausdauersport-Coaches weltweit die Arbeit 31.03.2026 Letzte Woche lancierte das Zürcher Startup augo seinem KI-Assistenten für Triathlon-, Lauf- und Rad-Coaches. Schon jetzt wird das Tool für das Training in Ausdauer-Sportarten international genutzt: Rund 150 Nutzerinnen und Nutzer aus verschiedenen Teilen der Welt haben die Plattform in der Beta-Phase getestet. Coaching lebt von individueller Betreuung. Mit der steigenden Zahl an Athletinnen und Athleten wächst jedoch auch der administrative Aufwand für Coaches: Trainingspläne liegen in einer App, die Kommunikation erfolgt über eine andere und Feedback ist über Kalendereinträge oder Chat-Verläufe verstreut – den Überblick zu behalten, ist entsprechend aufwendig. Dazu kommt, dass sich Remote-Coaching mehr und mehr zum Standard wird, was die Kommunikation weiter verkompliziert. Das Zürcher Startup augo will mit seinem KI-Tool Coaches die Arbeit erleichtern und die Kommunikation mit den Athletinnen und Athleten unterstützen. Augo wurde 2025 von den Trainerinnen Bruna Maia und Fabienne Maia sowie dem Triathleten Andreas Hermann gegründet. Das Tool bündelt Kommunikation, Trainingsdaten und Feedback zu Trainingseinheiten an einem zentralen Ort und führt somit Informationen aus verschiedenen Quellen zusammen. Das Tool umfasst zudem einen KI-Assistenten für die Coaches: Sie können Fragen zum Trainingsverlauf stellen, sich Zusammenhänge aufzeigen lassen und sich gezielt auf Gespräche vorbereiten. Dadurch erhält ein Coach gezielte Einsichten in diese Zusammenhänge und verbringt nicht mehr Zeit damit, diese Informationen selbst durch das Durchscrollen von Trainingskalendern und Wechseln von Apps zu finden. Für die Nutzung zahlen sie eine monatliche Gebühr. Athletinnen und Athleten benutzen das Tool kostenlos mit ihrem Coach. Sie erhalten Feedback zu ihren Trainingseinheiten und können auch selbst Rückmeldungen geben – etwa zu Energielevel, Schmerzen und Ernährung. So entsteht ein kontinuierlicher Austausch und Fortschritte bleiben für beide Seiten nachvollziehbar. «Wir haben augo nicht entwickelt, weil wir glauben, dass Coaching automatisiert werden sollte. Die menschliche Verbindung ist unersetzlich – aber die manuelle Arbeit, die Coaches davon abhält, sich darauf zu konzentrieren, ist es nicht», so Bruna Maia, Co-Founderin von augo. Aktuell testen 150 Nutzerinnen und Nutzer die Plattform und weitere 100 stehen auf der Warteliste. Der Ansatz ist von Anfang an international ausgerichtet: Zu den Nutzerinnen zählen unter anderem die US-amerikanische ehemalige Pro-Runnerin Nell Rojas und der aus Italien stammende Sportwissenschaftler und Coach Marco Altini. Neben den USA und Italien stammen die Testnutzerinnen und -nutzer aus Kanada, Brasilien und Europa. Die Plattform ist derzeit in Englisch, Deutsch und Portugiesisch verfügbar, weitere Sprachen sind geplant. Der offizielle Launch erfolgte letzte Woche. Gleichzeitig bereitet das augo-Team eine Fundraising-Runde vor. Bisher wurde augo eigenständig finanziert, eine erste Finanzierungsrunde soll im April starten. (Press release / LF) Bild: (v.l.n.r) Fabienne Maia, Bruna Maia, Andreas Hermann / ZVG 0 Comments LAUNCH More news about augo training AG Related tags

Title: Five deep-tech pioneers shortlisted for the ZKB Pionierpreis Technopark 2026
Article_Text: Five deep-tech pioneers shortlisted for the ZKB Pionierpreis Technopark 2026 31.03.2026 Five Swiss startups working in photonics, robotics, pharmaceutical technology and renewable energy storage have been named finalists for the ZKB Pionierpreis Technopark 2026. The finalists are competing for a CHF 100,000 top prize at one of Switzerland’s leading deep-tech awards and will present their technologies live on May 12 at Technopark Zurich. The jury of the ZKB Pionierpreis Technopark has selected the five finalists for the 2026 edition of one of Switzerland’s leading deep-tech startup awards. The decision was made during the last jury meeting chaired by Lothar Thiele, who presided over the jury for the last time this year. Chosen from a shortlist of 13 nominated startups, the selected finalists represent some of the most promising deep-tech ventures emerging from Switzerland. Interest in the Pionierpreis continues to grow. The 2026 edition attracted 76 applications, marking the highest level of participation in the award’s history. Following an intensive selection process, the jury has now shortlisted the following five finalists. The Five Finalists (in alphabetical order) Ccraft SA (Neuchâtel) Ccraft addresses a growing bottleneck in the data infrastructure required for the AI era. High-performance data centres increasingly rely on optical communication, yet scalable production of energy-efficient photonic chips remains limited. As an independent foundry specialising in Thin-Film Lithium Niobate (TFLN) technology, Ccraft manufactures high-performance photonic chips in Switzerland that convert electrical into optical signals with exceptional efficiency. The technology enables faster and more energy-efficient data transmission, helping build the next generation of sustainable AI infrastructure. Glycoera AG (Zurich) Glycoera is developing a new therapeutic approach for autoimmune diseases, where existing treatments often lack precision and produce limited clinical benefits. The startup’s in vivo glycoengineering platform enables the development of novel biologics that bind disease-causing proteins through a natural mechanism of the liver. These proteins are then selectively degraded, offering a targeted and potentially more effective treatment strategy in immunology. Roboa AG (Zurich) Roboa tackles one of the most complex challenges in industrial inspection and rescue operations: accessing extremely confined or hazardous environments. Its highly flexible modular snake robots can navigate narrow pipes, closed tanks or collapsed structures that remain inaccessible to humans or conventional robotic systems. The technology enables inspection, maintenance and emergency response in environments previously considered unreachable. Rtdt Laboratories AG (Zurich) Wind turbines often operate below their potential because the aerodynamic behaviour of rotor blades cannot be measured precisely during operation. With Aerosense, the startup has developed an innovative sensor patch that analyses rotor aerodynamics in real time. The system enables operators to optimise turbine control settings for maximum site-specific energy output, improving both efficiency and reliability in wind energy production. Unbound Potential AG (Zurich) The transition to renewable energy requires scalable and affordable energy storage solutions. Current technologies remain expensive and difficult to scale locally. Unbound Potential has developed a membrane-free flow battery designed to deliver cost-effective and rapidly scalable energy storage. The technology enables flexible, locally producible storage solutions that support a resilient and sustainable energy infrastructure. “The quality of this year’s nominees is exceptionally high,” says Lothar Thiele, Chairman of the Jury. “We are seeing scientifically grounded technologies with clear commercial potential, combined with remarkable thematic diversity. This breadth reflects the growing maturity and strength of Switzerland’s deep-tech ecosystem.” Award ceremony: innovation meets perseverance All five finalists will present their innovations live during the award ceremony at Technopark Zurich. In addition to the CHF 100,000 main prize, two further finalists will receive CHF 10,000 each. An Audience Award worth CHF 5,000 will also be presented during the event. “At the Pionierpreis, we look beyond technology alone.” says Michelle Tschumi, jury member and Head of Startup Finance at Zürcher Kantonalbank. “What inspires us most are founders who combine curiosity with perseverance and a genuine commitment to creating societal value. Particularly compelling are solutions that bridge cutting-edge research with real-world application.” The 2026 award ceremony will take place under the guiding theme “Perseverance”. In her keynote, philosopher, journalist and presenter Barbara Bleisch will reflect on perseverance and the human experience of failure. The event will be moderated by Tobias Müller, returning to the role after a three-year break. Title: 10 scale-ups selected for the first Project Switzerland batch
Article_Text: 10 scale-ups selected for the first Project Switzerland batch 31.03.2026 Project Switzerland pairs promising founders who already have a strong track record with a group of more than 20 of Switzerland’s most successful entrepreneurs. The 10 selected companies were narrowed down from 160 nominations and 60 applications through a rigorous, multi-stage process involving deep-dive interviews with veteran entrepreneurs and ecosystem experts. While Switzerland consistently ranks as a world champion in innovation, building a pipeline of Switzerland's next global giants like Nestlé, ABB, or Logitech requires significant scaling expertise. Project Switzerland, initiated by the Deep Tech Nation Switzerland Foundation, wants to provide this by bringing founders with experienced entrepreneurs. "Switzerland is a world champion in innovation, but our biggest challenge remains the growth gap. We initiated Project Switzerland to bridge the scaling expertise of our most successful entrepreneurs with the next generation of founders, securing Switzerland’s technological sovereignty and long-term prosperity", comments Joanne Sieber, CEO Deep Tech Nation Switzerland Today, Project Switzerland officially announced the 10 high-growth Scale-ups selected for its first-ever national initiative. The companies are Corintis - develops microfluidic cooling systems integrated directly into high-performance silicon chips. With around 75 engineers across 20+ nationalities, the majority of leading AI chip makers as customers and $60M raised since inception, the company is scaling fast. Futurae - provides seamless authentication and fraud prevention solutions for financial institutions. With 15M+ users on the platform, 400M+ authorized actions per year, and near-zero churn, Futurae is now expanding its platform into digital identity and into agentic AI authorization. Insolight - creates agrivoltaic systems that combine solar energy production with farming. Insolight is growing fast with 12 projects built and more than 70 projects secured with farmers across Europe, representing CHF 250 M. In 2026 the agritech plans to reach CHF 10 M in revenues and break even in terms of EBITDA. KEMARO - manufactures autonomous robotic sweepers for industrial dry-cleaning of large warehouses. The company generates more than $10 million (LTM) revenue and achieved +300% revenue growth in the US by 2025. Largo - uses artificial intelligence to predict the commercial success of films. Trusted by many industry giants like Sony Pictures, Disney, Mars, and Intuit, Largo.ai is backed by high-profile investors including Sylvester Stallone and Thomas Tippl. Ledgy - operates an equity management platform for cap tables and stock option plans. From FTSE 100 corporations and Tier 1 management consultancies to some of Europe’s most valuable private companies, Ledgy serves 800+ clients across 40+ jurisdictions. Backed by Sequoia Capital and New Enterprise Associates, Ledgy has raised $33.5M, is profitable, and is growing at ~60% year-over-year with a team of around 75 people- Neustark - captures and stores carbon dioxide permanently within recycled demolition concrete. Neustark is currently scaling up across the globe. With a team of 55, Neustark has already 47 capture and storage sites in operation. Pexapark - provides software and data for renewable energy sales and risk. Pexapark has facilitated over 45,000 megawatts of transactions and today serves over 250 customers across Europe and the US. QUMEA - offers contactless mobility monitoring systems to detect patient falls risk. QUMEA is the European market leader in digital mobility monitoring, deployed in more than 150 healthcare institutions across the DACH region and the Nordics. Voltiris - develops solar modules for greenhouses that harvest unused light wavelengths. The company has completed 14+ projects across Europe, and has ongoing collaborations in Asia, and the Middle East. Voltiris’ active development pipeline extends to Korea, China, Australia, and the Americas. The cohort enters the program with a combined CHF 270.2 million in funding, a total of 550 employees and a collective revenue of CHF 56.6 million for 2025, growing at an impressive average year-over-year rate of 133%. "These 10 companies have built an incredibly strong foundation, both in their technology and their business models", says Michael Sauter, from Deep Tech Nation Switzerland. "By connecting them with seasoned entrepreneurs who have already successfully navigated the complexities of global expansion, we're turning personal lessons into a collective strength for the entire Swiss ecosystem". The 12-month program provides these founders with direct access to Switzerland’s best Role Models. "As part of the selection committee, what stood out to me was the level of ambition in this cohort", adds Kevin Sartori, entrepreneur and Role Model. "They aren't content with being local champions; they have the drive to build businesses that give Switzerland global leverage. We are here to ensure they have the 'deep bench' of expertise to get there". Title: Marco Odermatt investiert in Obwaldner Startup
Article_Text: Marco Odermatt investiert in Obwaldner Startup 30.03.2026 Wie der Tages-Anzeiger berichtet, steigt Skistar Marco Odermatt neu als Investor bei Gaisbock ein. Damit folgt er dem Beispiel anderer prominenter Spitzensportler wie Dario Cologna, Lara Gut-Behrami oder Wendy Holdener, die sich ebenfalls unternehmerisch engagieren. Marco Odermatt, Olympiasieger und fünfmaliger Gesamtweltcupsieger in Folge, zählt zu den erfolgreichsten Schweizer Skirennfahrern. Nun bringt der Nidwaldner seine Erfahrung aus dem Spitzensport auch in die Wirtschaft ein: Laut Tages-Anzeiger beteiligt er sich am Sarner Startup Gaisbock. Sein Anteil liegt zwischen zehn und zwanzig Prozent. Gaisbock entwickelt natürliche Pflege- und Kosmetikprodukte mit Schwerpunkt auf Männerpflege. Ergänzend bietet das 2023 gegründete Unternehmen Seren für Sportlerinnen und Sportler an, die unter anderem die Regeneration nach körperlicher Belastung unterstützen sollen. Odermatt wird in Zukunft die Produkte testen und Rückmeldung zu deren Wirkung geben. Die Produkte werden in der eigenen Manufaktur in Büron im Luzerner Hinterland entwickelt und produziert. Sie sind im Sportfachhandel sowie in Apotheken, Drogerien und online erhältlich. Geschäftsführer Ramon Riebli und Marco Odermatt kennen sich noch aus ihrer gemeinsamen Zeit an der Sportschule Engelberg – nun arbeiten sie erstmals auch geschäftlich zusammen. Odermatt ist nicht der einzige Spitzensportler, der in Unternehmen investiert. Unter den Olympiasiegerinnen und Olympiasiegern aus der Wintersportwelt finden sich zahlreiche prominente Beispiele: Der Langläufer Dario Cologna ist am Getränke-Startup KAEX beteiligt, ebenso wie Lara Gut-Behrami. Auch die beiden Skirennfahrerinnen Wendy Holdener und Michelle Gisin engagieren sich als Investorinnen beim Sportnahrungs-Startup Nutriathletic. (Press release / LF) Bild: Gaisbock Shampoo / via Gaisbock.ch 0 Comments FINANCING More news about Gaisbock AG Related tags

Title: Tech4Regen 2026 unveils new 15 deeptech cohort
Article_Text: Tech4Regen 2026 unveils new 15 deeptech cohort 27.03.2026 The EPFL Innovation Park led program kicked off with a launch event on March 24, which brought together founders, coaches, mentors, and strategic partners. This session marked the start of a collaborative journey aimed at defining clear, measurable goals and accelerating the development of effective solutions in the coming months. This year’s cohort is tackling critical challenges across three key sectors: Nature & Agri-Tech Digit Soil (Schlieren) vision is to bridge the gap between environmental health and economic viability in the context of agriculture. The startup tackles the over-fertilization by unlocking the timing of nutrient release, providing farmers data-driven, actionable recommendations. rrreef (Zurich) vision is to regenerate our dying coral reefs globally, regenerating reefs as critical infrastructure that protects coasts, improves ocean health, and sustains coastal communities. The startups wants to help coral reefs survive the next 30 years. BCHAR (Zurich) emerged from research at an ETH Zurich association with the ambition to help solve climate change by building biochar CDR infrastructure that transforms agricultural residues into regenerative soil inputs and long-term climate impact at scale. BCHAR’s mission is to scale carbon removal by making biochar production accessible, efficient, and impactful. The company aims to empower businesses to transform agricultural waste into a valuable tool for carbon removal and soil restoration. Nettle Circle AG (Oberkirch) envisions regional nettle hubs across Europe turning a once ignored “weed” into mainstream fiber, replacing imported cotton & synthetics, restoring soils, stabilizing farmer income & helping brands meet tightening EU climate/due diligence laws. The startup builds & franchises weather independent nettle hubs in Europe that connect farmers, machinery partners & mills to turn whole nettle plants into circular fibers & by products, giving textile brands local supply that cuts imports & meets new laws. Techponics (Fribourg) builds automated, software-driven hydroponic systems that allow growers to produce leafy greens more efficiently. By combining robotics, intelligent plant spacing, and crop monitoring, the startup’s solution increases productivity while reducing labor, water use, and operational complexity. Their mission is to empower leafy-greens growers with automated, data-driven production systems that increase productivity, reduce labor dependency, and make large-scale sustainable farming economically viable. Sixteen44 (Lausanne): Methane is a powerful driver of global warming, trapping over 80x more heat than CO2 over 20 years. Though short-lived, rising methane levels are accelerating near-term climate change, pushing the planet closer to dangerous tipping points. Sixteen44 develops technology that reduces global warming by destroying atmospheric methane. Its mission is to remove 1 million tons of methane (equivalent to ~80 million tons of CO2e) from the air every year by 2035. SimplexDNA (Winterthur): Organizations face rising pressure to report biodiversity impacts but lack scalable, affordable data solutions. eDNA enables fast, low-cost assessments, yet most eDNA data remains inaccessible, limiting informed, nature-positive decisions. We build a centralized global eDNA data layer, transforming fragmented datasets into a unified, ready-to-use resource. Accessible via web and API, it provides reliable biodiversity data for reporting, compliance, and decision-making at scale. Clean & Circular Manufacturing PeelPack AG (Zurich) mission is to scale a circular system that turns agricultural side streams into sustainable materials and makes fossil-free packaging the new standard. Circea SA (Mies): was founded to turn wasted biogenic CO2 emissions from fermentation and biogas processes into valuable products, closing the carbon loop and replacing fossil CO2 in agri-food and chemical industries. Circea combines decentralized CO2 recovery technologies with local, circular ecosystems that enable the reuse of biogenic CO2, ensuring traceable, reliable and cost-effective carbon flows while strengthening supply-chain resilience. DeltaSpark (Lausanne): On top of being a major environmental issue, due to carbon tax, CO2 is also becoming a major financial problem for industries. DeltaSpark sees more in CO2 that just a costly waste. Using electrolysis, the startup turns CO2 into economic opportunities such as clean H2, building materials and green chemicals. Evolium Technologies (Sion) was founded by Dr. A. Staub to address lithium-ion battery waste and the need for affordable energy storage. Driven by circular economy principles, Evolium transforms used batteries into repairable, capacity-guaranteed systems. Evolium develops automated processes to give lithium-ion batteries a second life and delivers products for reliable energy storage with guaranteed capacity, enabling a circular battery value chain and long-term performance for renewable energy. Renewable Energy Systems GraphEnergyTech (Ecublens): Clean energy technologies still rely on scarce, costly and carbon-intensive metals. Founded between EPFL and the University of Cambridge, its team is driven to remove this bottleneck by enabling truly scalable, affordable and sustainable energy hardware. The mission is to accelerate the decarbonization of energy systems by enabling manufacturers to move away from critical metals, through industrially compatible alternatives that reduce cost, emissions and supply-chain risk. Sunaxer SA (Ecublens) was created to accelerate the decarbonization of industrial heat. Responsible for 20% of global emissions, and equaling worldwide electricity consumption, industrial heat is the largest overlooked emission source today. Sunaxer accelerates the adoption of solar process heat through its innovative receivers for concentrating solar collectors. Concentrating collectors generate process heat in the 100°-400°C range which encompasses half of the industrial heat demand. WattAnyWhere (Sion) unlocks clean, renewable power anywhere, anytime. As grids reach their limits, upgrading becomes slow and costly. The company empowers datacenters, e mobility providers and retailers with fast, flexible, high resilience energy access. It solves this time-to-power issue and help businesses or government entities increase their energy autonomy and transition away from CO2 loaded grid or diesel gensets. Its pollutant-free genset produces clean electricity out of renewable ethanol. EAPOSYS (Biel) unlocks geothermal everywhere by drilling for heat instead of hydrocarbons, enabling safe and sovereign energy infrastructure and a fair transition to a post-carbon economy. EAPOSYS’ mission is to accelerate the deployment of geothermal energy at scale and everywhere, by enabling safe, predictable and financeable projects that replace fossil fuels with reliable, local baseload energy worldwide. Driven by innovation and sustainability The selected startups reflect a strong commitment to innovation and sustainability, with solutions spanning soil health, circular materials, and renewable energy infrastructure. Contacted by Startupticker.ch, Tech4Regen Program Lead Bertrand Klaiber tells us more: “What stands out in this third Tech4Regen cohort is not just the quality of the startups and their solutions, but the passion and shared ambition of the people behind them—entrepreneurs, mentors, and industry experts coming together to shape an economy that thrives within planetary boundaries.” Tech4Regen is a unique 8-month, equity-free acceleration program based at EPFL Innovation Park, accelerating the impact of purpose-driven startups. Title: UK patient data platform Sanius Health acquires Kielo Research
Article_Text: UK patient data platform Sanius Health acquires Kielo Research 27.03.2026 Sanius Health, a leading real-world evidence and patient-generated data company focused on rare and underserved conditions, has acquired Zug-based Kielo Research. Founded in 2022, the company focusses on integrating the patient perspective into the development and evaluation of medicines, medical technologies and healthcare services. Kielo Research is recognised for its deep expertise in patient preference studies, patient-reported outcomes and qualitative research that captures how patients experience diseases and treatments. These insights enable healthcare organisations to design more effective therapies, clinical trials and care pathways by ensuring patient perspectives are meaningfully incorporated throughout the development process. By combining Kielo’s expertise in patient-centred research with Sanius Health’s secure research infrastructure, advanced analytics and patient engagement capabilities, Sanius will expand its ability to generate high-quality evidence that reflects real patient experiences and priorities. It also expands Sanius’ expertise across a broad set of therapeutic areas, including cardiovascular disease, oncology, respiratory medicine, metabolic disease, neurology, dermatology and vaccine development. Sanius Health operates across the United States, the United Kingdom and Asia, working with healthcare providers, patients, researchers and life sciences companies to support better care and the development of treatments through the responsible use of health insights, clinical expertise and advanced analytics. Through its rare disease and oncology registries, Sanius Health supports clinical trial recruitment, regulatory validation and reimbursement pathways, connecting healthcare providers, researchers and life sciences organisations through established research partnerships. Combined with integration across one of the largest primary care provider footprints in the United Kingdom, the platform supports patient management, care delivery, pathway redesign, cost improvement and accelerated clinical trials, while generating decision-grade evidence for regulators, payers and life sciences partners. The platform holds particular strength in rare diseases and haematology, with programmes across sickle cell disease (SCD), myeloproliferative neoplasms (MPNs), multiple myeloma (MM), Waldenström’s macroglobulinaemia (WM), chronic myeloid leukaemia (CML) and chronic lymphocytic leukaemia (CLL). Tommi Tervonen, Chief Scientist & Founder of Kielo Research, comments: “We are delighted to join Sanius Health. Both organisations share a strong commitment to ensuring that the patient voice is heard and reflected in the development of new treatments and healthcare solutions. By combining Kielo’s expertise in patient-centred research with Sanius Health’s technology and global research capabilities, we will generate deeper insights into patient experiences and preferences.” (Press release / SK) 0 Comments FINANCING More news about Kielo Research GmbH Related tags

Title: HSG START Accelerator launches second batch
Article_Text: HSG START Accelerator launches second batch 27.03.2026 The HSG START Accelerator officially introduced its second batch during START Summit 2026. This time, eight startups from the fields of robotics, health tech, climate tech, AI, and hardware are joining the program. The second edition of the HSG START Accelerator program officially kicked off just before this year’s START Summit. The accelerator's goal is to prepare European startups in their early growth phase for scaling through professional support and a dedicated program. The selected startups are Aithon Robotics (Zurich) – Developing drones for safer and more efficient inspections and maintenance work in height ExoSphere (Munich) – Enhancing survival rates of cancer patients through early detection via a blood sample FireDrone (Zurich) – Developing drones for extreme heat and hazardous environments for emergency response and industrial inspection goNEON (Zurich) – Enabling compliant planning of infrastructure and transportation in cities with an agentic AI solution Lightlink Instruments (St. Gallen) – Developing a hyperspectral camera for pollution monitoring, display color measurement, earth observation and more NoxBlanc (St. Gallen) – Personalising sleep with an adjustable mattress combining firmness and temperature control. optohive (Zurich) – Building next-generation wearable brain imaging technology that enables more efficient and cost-effective research, accelerates clinical trials, and supports earlier diagnosis and better treatment outcomes for patients with neurological disorders. SurfAce Cleantech (Lausanne) – Empowering compostable packaging with advanced coating technology that boosts performance, cutting costs and making sustainable solutions truly competitive. Over the coming months, the teams will take part in a structured program that includes mentoring, access to a strong network, and hands-on support in further developing their business models. The program will conclude with a Demo Day on May 12, 2026, where the participating startups will present their DeepTech innovations to the investor community. Title: 1,000 deeptech companies launched with Venture Kick
Article_Text: 1,000 deeptech companies launched with Venture Kick 27.03.2026 With the founding of Stellar Alpina (in-space transportation), Venture Kick reaches a major milestone: its 1,000th startup has emerged from the program. In total, the supported companies created over 16,000 jobs and attracted more than CHF 10 billion in follow-on financing. Since 2007, Venture Kick has supported teams from academia and research in transforming innovation into market-ready companies. A total of 1,286 startup projects have been funded with CHF 103 million. This has led to the creation of 1,000+ deeptech companies, which today employ more than 16,000 people and have attracted over CHF 10.2 billion in follow-on investments. These figures highlight the strong leverage effect of early-stage funding. With comparatively limited resources, substantial economic value is generated. At the same time, the portfolio demonstrates high stability. With a survival rate of 84 percent, Venture Kick underscores the sustainable development of its supported companies. “Who would have thought that a vision launched in 2007 to foster new company creation would lead to 1,000 companies,” says Beat Schillig, Co-Managing Director of Venture Kick. “Each of these companies shows how research can be translated into economic value by creating jobs, attracting international investment and contributing in tangible ways to Switzerland’s economy and society.” Over the long term, these startup companies make a significant contribution to employment in Switzerland. They employ 19 people on average today. Companies that have been on the market for more than 10 years employ today more than 37 people on average and continue to show strong growth. This development is also reflected in increasing diversity. 42 percent of supported projects have at least one woman in the founding team. This highlights the growing role of women in science-based entrepreneurship in Switzerland. Looking ahead, Venture Kick aims to support a total of 3,000 startups by 2035, generating up to CHF 50 billion follow-on investments. Already in 2026, the number of supported projects is expected to increase by 15 percent, enabling more teams to bring their innovations to market. More number can be found in the Venture Kick magazine 2025. 0 Comments SUPPORT Principal Investments 2026 The Swiss Venture Capital Report 2026 offers key insights into the latest trends shaping the investment landscape. Title: Switzerland could be more innovation-friendly
Article_Text: Switzerland could be more innovation-friendly 26.03.2026 A new study by the State Secretariat for Education, Research and Innovation (SERI) shows how long-standing structures are hampering the rise of start-ups. These include regulation, as well as funding opportunities and established companies that prefer to collaborate with other established firms rather than with start-ups. Similar factors also hold SMEs back. A more innovation-friendly environment would help both. When it comes to innovation, there are two opposing trends in Switzerland. On the one hand, a thriving start-up ecosystem has emerged over the last 20 years, which is growing steadily and gaining in importance. At the same time, however, the innovation activity of all companies is concentrated in a smaller number of firms. A recent study by the State Secretariat for Education, Research and Innovation (SERI) sheds light on these trends, based on a survey of more than 1,100 companies and complemented by two Delphi rounds. Startups a key innovation driver Out of all the respondents to the survey, 9.2% are startups. These include all companies younger than 10 years which identified themselves as a university or public research organization spin-off, a corporate split-off or an independent startup. According to the report, startups play an important role for Switzerland’s innovation capacity. 68.4% of startups report R&D activity—significantly more than both older firms (59.9%) and younger non-startups (37.4%). However, this result should be interpreted with caution. As stated, the study relies on a non-representative sample with a strong focus on innovation-active sectors, meaning that highly innovative firms are likely overrepresented. These sectors include: Chemicals, pharmaceuticals & biotech; ICT; medtech; metals, electronics, machines; Food and Finance. Startups attract public support Startups are disproportionately supported by public instruments: 22.2% of the startups receive public innovation support, and 28.7% benefit from Innosuisse funding—far higher shares than among other young non-startups, of which 2.8% attract public innovation support and 9.3% Innosuisse support. Collaborations Established companies, on the other hand, remain sceptical of start-ups. The authors of the study find that startups are not seen as an important player by other companies in innovation collaboration. Only 40.6% of respondents said startups are important. More important were established companies and academic partners. 76.8% of respondents said established companies were important, while 72.3% emphasized collaboration with academic partners. Regulation hurdles At the same time, there are other factors within the start-up environment that prevent them from realising their full potential and demonstrate that the Swiss economy as a whole is still lagging behind the growing importance of start-ups. Regulation can foster innovation because if clear, stable and well-communicated, it creates safe market environments, creating trust among market participants. However, many participants in the Delphi rounds complained about excessive regulatory burden and bureaucracy in Switzerland. Three issues stand out: One-size-fits-all approaches that impose disproportionate burdens on startups and SMEs Fragmentation across cantons, which increases compliance costs and slows market entry A generally risk-averse regulatory culture, which tends to favor incremental over radical innovation Taken together, these factors may not prevent innovation—but they can significantly slow it down, especially in early and experimental stages. Furthermore, participants in the Delphi rounds emphasized the well-known bottlenecks in later-stage funding, as well as the difficulties in attracting and retaining highly qualified employees, especially in technical fields. Policy implications The authors have compiled various policy recommendations to address the above issues: More flexible regulation Regulatory sandboxes could allow startups to test new business models under simplified conditions. At the same time, greater harmonization across cantons could reduce administrative friction and accelerate time-to-market. Strengthening growth financing Tax incentives for high-risk investments—similar to schemes in the UK—could mobilize additional private capital. Public-private co-investment funds or matching mechanisms may further help close the scale-up funding gap. Improving access to talent Fast-track procedures for highly skilled workers could ease recruitment constraints. Given strong domestic demand, international talent will likely remain a key component of Switzerland’s innovation system. SME and start-ups in the same boat The same factors that stifle innovation hinder both start-ups and SMEs. Whilst they have a negative impact on scaling for start-ups, they tend to bring innovation activities to a halt for SMEs. Start-ups therefore do not benefit from SMEs’ weaknesses in terms of innovation, but are in the same boat with them. Improvements in regulation, financing and access to talent would help both types of business. (Benjamin Klavins / Stefan Kyora) 0 Comments BACKGROUND Related tags

Title: Swiss Startup Association names new CEO
Article_Text: Swiss Startup Association names new CEO 25.03.2026 Michael Stucky, a co-founder of two ETH spin-offs and a decade-long figure in Swiss venture capital and innovation, has been named CEO of the Swiss Startup Association, effective April 7. The Swiss Startup Association represents over 1,800 members and advocates for improved conditions for startups in Switzerland, including better access to capital, equity participation schemes and streamlined administrative processes. Michael Stucky will take up the role of CEO for the Swiss Startup Association on the 7th of April 2026, bringing broad innovation experience. Over the past ten years, he has held roles in venture capital, start-ups, and innovation, while also co-founding two ETH spin-offs (exoskeleton company Auxivo and vaccine startup Glycovaxyn), taking on senior positions in the innovation and financial sectors, and supporting numerous founders as a coach and mentor. He will take over this role from the current CEO, Regula Bleuler. For Michael, the focus of his new role is clear: making an impact. He says that the Swiss Startup Association is now an important voice for start-ups in Switzerland, and it is precisely this mandate that he wants to further strengthen together with the members, partners, and the board. The appointment comes at a time when start-ups in Switzerland are more reliant than ever on a strong policy framework, political attention, and a well-connected advocacy group. The Swiss Startup Association has already published the Startup Agenda Schweiz and intends to continue playing an active role in strengthening these efforts, whether through political engagement, raising the profile of the ecosystem, or connecting founders with the business community, the political sphere, and society at large. With Michael Stucky at the helm, the association aims to further expand this role in the coming years. (Press release/AJ) Picture: Michael Stucky 0 Comments BACKGROUND Related tags

Title: «Digital Shapers 2026» puts 100 digital visionaries in the spotlight
Article_Text: «Digital Shapers 2026» puts 100 digital visionaries in the spotlight 25.03.2026 After a two-year hiatus, BILANZ, Handelszeitung, PME and digitalswitzerland have once again published a list of one hundred individuals who are significantly shaping digital Switzerland. The eighth edition once again features numerous start-up founders. Today, several technology waves are converging: AI, robotics, quantum computing, and the digitalization of critical infrastructures are developing in parallel and beginning to reinforce each other. Thank to years of investment in talent, infrastructure, and research, Switzerland plays a leading role in this second wave of digitalisation. The «Digital Shapers 2026» reflect this breadth through its ten categories. This year’s categories are: The AI Acrobats, the Androids (robots and drones), the Cloudriders, the Defenders (security and privacy), the Energizers (energy generation, storage, and efficient usage), the Fintechies, the Health Techies, the Quantum Leapers, the Frameworkers (politics, administration, and associations), the Mentors (consultants, trainers, VCs, or evangelists). The list once again features numerous founders. The proportion of start-ups is particularly high in the ‘Androids’ and ‘Energizers’ categories. Overall, however, there are fewer start-ups on the list than in previous editions. Furthermore, the list mainly features CEOs and founders of more established scale-ups, such as Andy Yen of Proton, Péter Fankhauser of Anybotics and Sabrina Schenardi of BLP Digital. There are only a few exceptions. These include Lara Gervaise, CEO & Co-Founder of Virtuosis AI. As in previous years, a member of the Startupticker Board has made it onto the Digital Shapers list. This time it is Roger Wüthrich-Hasenböhler, who also serves as a board member and on the advisory boards of various robot start-ups. A special issue «Digital Shapers 2026» will be published following yesterday’s evening event. The 108-page magazine, featuring portraits of the honorees, their work, and their motivation, will be included with the print editions of BILANZ, Handelszeitung, and PME. The list including the 100 portraits is also available online. (Press release / SK) Picture: TyliJura / pixabay 0 Comments BACKGROUND Related tags

Title: Riverkin, Orthosens and Tinamu win pitching competitions at START Summit 2026
Article_Text: Riverkin, Orthosens and Tinamu win pitching competitions at START Summit 2026 24.03.2026 Three Swiss startups won pitching competitions at the 2026 START Summit in St. Gallen. Riverkin took home a combined prize of CHF 50,000 in cash and legal credits for its water monitoring platform, while healthtech Orthosens and drone automation company Tinamu each won their respective competitions without cash prizes. In parallel, Tobias Reichmuth was awarded founder of the year by St. Gallen University. Innovation rarely happens in stable conditions. Over the past years, Europe's tech ecosystem has been shaped by disruption, volatility, and rapid shifts in technology. Under these conditions, START Summit, Europe's largest student-run startup conference, chose the guiding theme of “Embrace New Horizons” for 2026: a call to move beyond short-term thinking and actively shape what comes next. This year’s START Summit brought together over 7'000 attendees over two days, more than 1'500 startups and over 1'000 investors. The event featured an expanded slate of pitching competitions, and three Swiss start-ups were on the wining stages: Riverkin (founded 2024, Zürich) won the Swiss Sustainable Impact Award. The startup is an ETH spin-off developing a Water Data Ecosystem (WDE), featuring Smart Sensors that were designed during the founder's doctoral studies. The platform combines advanced sensors with data analytics to monitor water quality and quantity across entire watersheds, providing insights to better manage water resources, reduce pollution and mitigate financial risks. Riverkin won a CHF 20’000 monetary prize and CHF 30’000 of legal credits from the Zürich-based law firm Lenz & Staehelin. Orthosens (2023, Biel/Bienne) won the Healthtech & Longevity Pitching competition organized by Creadd. The company has developed a battery-free sensor for orthopedic implants. This device streams real-time biomechanical data during recovery, allowing clinicians to detect complications earlier, tailor rehabilitation and monitor their patients’ progress objectively. No cash prize was awarded. ETH spin-off Tinamu (2018, Zürich) wins the Robotics Pitching Competition. The company provides an environment-independent, automated way for commodity inspection in warehouses. Tinamu uses standard drones and sensors, and equips them with a custom software stack to allow them to navigate autonomously in unknown terrain. There was no monetary prize awarded for this pitching competition. START Summiteer Award goes to ESTER Biotech German-based biotech company ESTER Biotech won 1st prize in the START Summiteer Pitching competition, taking home the total prize pool of CHF 260’000. The company is a Leipzig University spin-off developing new enzyme-based technologies that break down plastics into reusable components. ESTER Biotech says it will use the prize money to support its ongoing seed funding. University of St. Gallen names its founder of the year Serial entrepreneur and University of St. Gallen (HSG) graduate Tobias Reichmuth was named the “HSG Founder of the Year 2026” at the START Summit for his latest venture, Maximon, founded in 2021. Maximon is an incubator dedicated to longevity, and it has already built three companies in this sector: AVEA Life sells supplements, Ayun provides comprehensive health checks and Frieda Health supports women through menopause. Reichmuth says, “For me, the topic of longevity is closely linked to entrepreneurship: I am convinced that research in this field will not only improve our lives but also open up new markets. Enabling a healthy life through personalized prevention and preventing diseases from developing in the first place would significantly relieve the burden on the healthcare system in the long term.” (AJ) Never miss a deadline again Start alarms for award deadlines Receive tailor made mails with news about your awards 0 Comments AWARD More news about Maximon AG OrthoSens SA Riverkin AG Tinamu Labs AG Company profiles on startup.ch Maximon AG OrthoSens SA Riverkin AG Tinamu Labs AG Highlights Maximon AG Top 100 Swiss Startup Award 2013 Highlights Tinamu Labs AG Innosuisse Certificate Top 100 Swiss Startup Award 2021 Top 100 Swiss Startup Award 2022 Top 100 Swiss Startup Award 2023 Winner Venture Kick Principal Investments 2026 The Swiss Venture Capital Report 2026 offers key insights into the latest trends shaping the investment landscape. Title: New Climate VC VitaminC Raises $20 million
Article_Text: New Climate VC VitaminC Raises $20 million 24.03.2026 VitaminºC, a new venture capital firm, today announced the first close of its €18M ($20M) debut early-stage climate fund. Operating from Zurich and San Francisco, the firm invests in pre-seed and seed climate technology companies across Europe and America. Global climate tech funding has slowed, valuations have reset, and many generalist funds have deprioritized the sector. VitaminºC views this market dislocation as a defining opportunity to invest at more attractive valuations with stronger fundamentals. As the world moves toward 2°C warming, climate solutions are becoming economic infrastructure, yet only about 10% of global venture capital is currently allocated to climate and energy transition technologies, leaving a significant investment gap, says VitaminºC in a press release. Founded by Nathalie Moral (Zurich) and Sophie Lamparter (San Francisco), the firm invests in climate mitigation and human adaptation. "We're here to rewire climate venture capital," said Nathalie Moral, Founding Partner. "The first generation of climate technologies, such as wind and solar, is now cheaper than fossil fuels. Around 30% of the technologies needed to cut emissions faster are still pre-commercial. The biggest economic buildout of our generation is just getting started - and we're investing when others are pulling back." VitaminºC today announced the first closing of the fund at $20 million. The fund is backed by European entrepreneurial families and supported by successful climate-tech founders and experts including Jasmine Kent (CTO & Co-Founder, Dufour Aerospace & Daedalean - exited), and climate policy expert Florian Egli (TUM Munich, EU Environment Agency Committee). Connecting Europe and the US "Europe's deep tech is world-class, but many startups hit a scale cap and struggle to expand globally," said Sophie Lamparter, Founding partner. "We introduce founders to their first US customers, hires and investors”. And as US climate policy faces uncertainty, American founders are increasingly looking to Europe's stable regulatory environment. The bridge works both ways and we are the boots on the ground in both eco-systems." VitaminºC invests in climate mitigation and human adaptation targeting the largest climate levers: energy, food and agriculture and carbon removal. The fund invests €0.5M–€1.5M ($0.6–1.7M) at pre-seed and seed stage. Each investment must demonstrate the potential to mitigate or remove at least 100,000 tons of CO₂, or improve the resilience of 100,000 people within five years. The firm’s first investment is Satellites on Fire, an Argentine-U.S. startup using AI, satellite, and ground data to detect, monitor, and prevent wildfires. For VitaminºC, impact and returns are inseparable. Beyond capital, the firm actively connects portfolio companies with academic institutions for rigorous impact validation, funds non-dilutive grants to generate scientific evidence, and pilots blended capital models where venture and philanthropy work in tandem to accelerate the transition. Experienced founding partners Nathalie Moral works at the intersection of venture, philanthropy and policy. She was an early backer of Climeworks, Europe's first climate tech unicorn, and co-founded Clima Now to bridge all three capital types at once, earning the Female Innovation Forum Recognition Award in 2022. Her career spans PricewaterhouseCoopers, Bain and Company, and LGT Venture Philanthropy (today Lightrock). She holds a degree from the University of St. Gallen. Sophie Lamparter has spent 15 years helping European deep tech companies grow in the US. She began as a science and technology diplomat at swissnex San Francisco, built the first US team at MindMaze during its growth from university spinout to unicorn, and later co-founded DART Ventures to support founders entering the US market. She holds a degree from the University of Zurich and UC Berkeley. (Press release / SK) Picture: VitaminºC Founding Partners L-R: Sophie Lamparter, Nathalie Moral (VitaminºC, Florian Kalotay) 0 Comments FINANCING Related tags

Title: Continuing trend towards deep tech
Article_Text: Continuing trend towards deep tech 24.03.2026 Lakestar, Walden Catalyst, and Dealroom have published ‘The 2026 European Deeptech Report’ underscoring a record surge for Europe’s deep tech ecosystem. VC funding for Deep Tech rose to $20.3bn and made up an all time high of 32% of all VC investment. The report which excludes traditional life sciences provides detailed insights into various sectors, country performance and comparisons with the US and other topics. Investment into European deep tech reached $20.3 billion in 2025, accounting for a record 32% of all venture capital—more than double its share a decade ago. Unlike the broader tech market, which remains 53% below its 2021 peak, deep tech funding is down just 4%, highlighting sustained investor conviction. There are various factors driving this trend. The most significant of these is the rise of AI, which, firstly, is enabling new products across a range of fields – from software and robotics to computational biology; secondly, is driving significant demand for technologies providing higher computing power; and thirdly, is challenging traditional software business models. Regarding VC funding the Future of Compute segment saw the fastest expansion in 2025 in absolute numbers, with funding more than doubling to $2.5 billion (+115%), while Defence reached $1.8 billion (+125%). Novel AI attracted $3.4 billion (+27%), and Computational Biology and Chemistry grew to $1.1 billion (+88%). Space ($1.3B), Novel Robotics ($468M) and Novel Energy ($700M) round out the landscape. The sector-by-sector analysis points to a second driving force behind the trend towards deep tech. Added together, defence, security and resilience now accounts for 43% of all European deep tech funding, up from 20% in 2022, reflecting surging demand for sovereign capabilities. Money from the outside However, when it comes to investments, the region remains dependent on non-European VCs. 70% of late-stage funding comes from non-European investors, and startups raise smaller rounds and progress more slowly than their US counterparts. The US also dominates when it comes to exits. By value, the majority of M&A is acquired by US companies. Public market listings showed signs of recovery in the US and China for Deep Tech companies, while European public markets remained quiet in 2025. Overall, the report highlights a massive funding gap between Europe and the US when it comes to AI investment. Twelve times more money flowed into US companies than into European ones in 2025. The situation is less dire in all other deep tech sectors. Here, the amount invested in the US was twice as high as in Europe. Steady growth in Switzerland Switzerland performs relatively well compared with other European countries, although start-ups and investments in biotech and medtech companies were not taken into account. It is striking that Switzerland has seen very steady growth in deep tech investments in recent years, whereas other countries of a similar size, such as Sweden, Finland and the Netherlands, have experienced significant fluctuations. The report highlighted AI and robotics as particular areas of strength for Switzerland. Recommendations Deep tech is becoming central to Europe’s competitiveness and technological sovereignty. However, the ecosystem risks missing the full opportunity. To build a thriving European Deep Tech ecosystem, the authors identify four main challenges: The growth-stage funding gap: Beyond Series A there is not enough capital in Europe to fund businesses locally. As a result, companies raise smaller rounds, sell, or take capital from overseas that often results in a shift in the ‘geographic centre of mass’ of the business away from Europe. Fragmentation: Europe suffers from high-friction, fragmented regulation across countries while lacking the power of concentrated talent clusters. Researcher to Founder: More needs to be done to turn European research excellence into high quality startups. Risk appetite: European corporates and governments need to work more closely with startups and embrace risk to drive success. The European Deep Tech Report can be downloaded from the Dealroom website. (Stefan Kyora) Picture: LongTermHolder / pixabay 1 Comments BACKGROUND Related tags

Title: Umlaut.bio extends pre-seed round to EUR 3 million
Article_Text: Umlaut.bio extends pre-seed round to EUR 3 million 23.03.2026 Umlaut.bio, a German-Swiss biotech company developing first-in-biology tRNA-modifying small molecules, has extended its pre-seed financing to around EUR 3 million through the support of BaseLaunch. In addition, the start-up has strengthened its Advisory Board. Umlaut.bio is a pioneering biotech company focused on developing first-in-biology therapies targeting cancer and inflammatory diseases. Small molecules will be developed to inhibit key steps in tRNA modification synthesis, thereby preventing overactivation of a multitude of signaling pathways at scale. This hallmark of uncontrolled cell proliferation in cancer and autoimmune diseases cannot be addressed with chemotherapy or by targeting individual proteins. Making this new regulatory layer druggable has the promise of addressing the shortcomings of current cancer and autoimmune treatments. The company has been funded by HTGF and LBBW Venture Capital, and also supported by Ono Pharmaceutical and Eli Lilly through the Mission BioCapital Platinum Program and AbbVie through the Golden Ticket. BaseLaunch’s funding brings the pre-seed financing round to EUR 3 million. This addition not only helps to accelerate its pioneering science addressing tRNA modifications through the expertise of BaseLaunch but also highlights the potential of the approach through the approval by the industry partners of BaseLaunch. Umlaut will further strengthen its Scientific Advisory Board (SAB) with two world-leading experts. Prof. Dr. Oliver Distler is a renowned key opinion leader in the field of Systemic Sclerosis, driving preclinical research up to shaping the guidelines for patient treatment. He will support Umlaut in the development of its program in Systemic Sclerosis. Prof. Dr. Tsutomu Suzuki’s research centers on RNA biochemistry, especially on the biogenesis and function of tRNA modifications, and molecular mechanisms of protein synthesis. He is heading the Department of Chemistry and Biotechnology, University of Tokyo and is one of the highest cited tRNA researchers. Prof. Suzuki will complement the scientific excellence of Umlaut in spearheading the development of its first-in-biology small molecules that inhibit tRNA modification enzymes. Karsten Fischer, Ph.D., Chief Executive Officer at Umlaut comments: “With BaseLaunch and Prof. Distler and Prof. Suzuki, Umlaut was able to add significant expertise, as well as a top-tier network within the pharmaceutical industry to advance its preclinical and clinical development. With this, we have taken the next steps in developing therapeutic molecules based on this novel fundamental biological principle.” (Press release / SK) 0 Comments FINANCING SUPPORT More news about Umlaut.bio GmbH Principal Investments 2026 The Swiss Venture Capital Report 2026 offers key insights into the latest trends shaping the investment landscape.
