Title: EVis Bioscience successfully closes €740K pre-seed round on Capital Cell
Article_Text: EVis Bioscience successfully closes €740K pre-seed round on Capital Cell 20.03.2026 Zurich-based EVis Bioscience AG, an ETH Zurich spin-off developing next-generation RNA delivery systems, has raised €740,000 on Capital Cell, surpassing the original funding target. The funding strengthens EVis’s position in next-generation RNA therapeutics. The campaign attracted strong support from private and professional investors across Europe, reflecting growing confidence in EVis’s hybrid RNA delivery platform and its potential to advance targeted gene therapies. While gene therapies offer transformative potential for rare diseases and genetic disorders, the safe, precise, and scalable delivery of genetic material remains a critical bottleneck. EVis Bioscience addresses this challenge through EVis NanoVector, a proprietary platform that enables the efficient loading of RNA into extracellular vesicles (EVs), leveraging a natural delivery system with strong potential for precision, safety, and scalability. The company operates a hybrid business model combining platform licensing, R&D collaborations, and proprietary therapeutic development. EVis provides industry and academic partners with access to its technology through licensing and collaborative research programs, while simultaneously advancing its own therapeutic candidates for rare genetic diseases toward preclinical milestones. Strategic Momentum The successful raise builds on recent institutional validation, including the CHF 311,000 non-dilutive grant awarded by Innosuisse for an 18-month preclinical program in collaboration with the University of Zurich. Together, these milestones significantly reinforce EVis Bioscience’s scientific credibility and financial foundation. Elita Montanari, CTO, acting CEO and co-founder of EVis Bioscience said: “The successful closure of our €740k pre-seed round on Capital Cell, reaching 105.5% of our target, represents a significant milestone for EVis Bioscience. We are grateful to our investors for their trust and support. Together with the backing of Innosuisse, we are now well positioned to advance our platform toward the next key stage of development.” With the pre-seed round successfully completed, EVis Bioscience is positioned to advance its hybrid RNA delivery platform, expand its scientific team, strengthen preclinical development programs and prepare for future strategic financing rounds. (Press release / SK) 0 Comments FINANCING More news about EVIS Bioscience AG Company profiles on startup.ch EVIS Bioscience AG Related tags

Title: New fund and foundation to back founders with disabilities and neurodivergent minds
Article_Text: New fund and foundation to back founders with disabilities and neurodivergent minds 20.03.2026 The Global Inclusive Founders Fund was launched today at the Start Summit; together with an associated foundation (Giff), it focuses on high-potential founders who have navigated systemic barriers — including disability, neurodivergence, or chronic conditions. The initiative is backed by Swiss national councillor and former entrepreneur Islam Alijaj, serial entrepreneur and investor Hermann Arnold, and Louise Maunoir. Giff introduces a dual foundation–fund model. The initiative launches first as a Swiss philanthropic foundation, designed to identify and support high-potential founders who have navigated systemic barriers — including disability, neurodivergence, or chronic conditions. The Giff Foundation has already been legally established in Switzerland as a dependent foundation under the umbrella of Fondation des Fondateurs. In parallel, the team is building the fund part starting with an angel investment group and investment committee, with experienced investors including Daniel Gutenberg. The founding investor circle also includes Bettina Hein, serial entrepreneur and Swiss Shark Tank investor, Joachim Schoss, founder of Scout24, one of Europe’s first digital unicorns, and serial entrepreneur and angel investor Nicole Herzog. The ambition is to close first deals in Q2 2026. Selecting and supporting founders The foundation itself focuses on changing the status quo, helping identify talent early and supporting founders with disabilities to see entrepreneurship as a real path. This includes identifying promising founders, preparing them for accelerator programs, and building partnerships with accelerators so that inclusion becomes part of their innovation pipelines. The goal is to ensure that talented founders with disabilities or neurodivergent profiles have real access to the same entrepreneurial ecosystems and opportunities as any other high-potential founders. “Through the networks of our founders and partners, from Sigma Squared, Kickstart, START Global, EWOR and other accelerator communities, we aim to use every resource available to help these founders build the strongest possible networks”, explains Louise Maunoir. The foundation’s activities, including founder discovery, mentorship, and ecosystem access, are financed through philanthropic partners and foundations that share our ambition to drive systemic change in entrepreneurship. “We are fortunate to already be supported by several foundations that believe in helping build a more inclusive innovation ecosystem”, says Maunoir. Investments in high-growth companies Unlike traditional impact funds, Giff does not invest in social enterprises. Instead, the focus is on high-growth, venture-scale companies built by founders whose experiences of exclusion have shaped exceptional resilience and perspective. The thesis behind Giff is simple: founders navigating physical and mental disabilities develop unusual problem-solving abilities, determination, and systems thinking qualities that translate into entrepreneurial advantage. Yet they remain dramatically underrepresented in venture capital funding. Too often, they grow up without role models showing that building a company is even possible. One example is Islam Alijaj, who grew up with a physical disability and speech impairment. After being sidelined by traditional systems, he went on to build and exit a company — an investment that would have returned more than 1000x for early backers. He later entered the Swiss Parliament, becoming one of Switzerland’s most prominent voices for inclusion. Founder discovery for the fund has already begun. “We are currently building a pan-European scouting network and have already have several promising founders and startups in our pipeline, identified across ecosystems such as London, San Francisco, and Munich”, says Maunoir. Title: Swiss biotechs advance clinical programs across multiple therapeutic areas
Article_Text: Swiss biotechs advance clinical programs across multiple therapeutic areas 19.03.2026 Five biotech companies announced recent developments ranging from FDA Fast Track and Orphan Drug designations to the start of Phase 1 and Phase 3 clinical trials. The updates highlight progress in oncology, rare blood disorders, neurodegenerative diseases, multidrug-resistant infections, and Duchenne muscular dystrophy. Pilatus Biosciences, a startup based in Vaud, is developing novel immunotherapies for liver and gastrointestinal cancers. Founded in 2022 from the Ludwig Institute for Cancer Research and supported by the Cancer Research Institute, Pilatus operates internationally with R&D teams in Switzerland and Taiwan. The company announced that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has granted Fast Track designation for its lead drug, PLT012, designed to treat liver cancer. The Fast Track status is intended to facilitate the development and expedite the review of therapies that treat serious conditions and address unmet medical needs. PLT012 is also being explored in other solid tumors and is being tested in combination with existing therapies. A Phase 1 clinical trial has already begun. In addition, Pilatus has received FDA Orphan Drug designation for PLT012 for liver and bile duct cancers. mAbTree Biologics receives Orphan Drug Designation Focused on next-generation immunology-driven biologics, Basel-based mAbTree Biologics announced that the FDA has granted Orphan Drug Designation for its lead investigational biologic, MT0002, targeting two rare blood cancers: Essential Thrombocythemia (ET) and Polycythaemia Vera (PV). Orphan Drug Designation is a special status the FDA grants to drugs being developed to treat rare diseases, defined as conditions affecting fewer than 200,000 people in the United States. Patients with ET and PV often require lifelong treatment, highlighting the need for new therapeutic options. MT0002 is being developed in collaboration with Shilpa Biologics, part of the Shilpa Medicare Group based in India. BioVersys Receives Green Light for Global Phase 3 Study The string of good news for BioVersys continues. The Basel‑based clinical‑stage biopharmaceutical company focuses on research and development of novel antibacterial products for serious, life‑threatening infections caused by multidrug‑resistant (MDR) bacteria. The company announced that the U.S. Food and Drug Administration (FDA) has given the green light for its lead candidate, BV100, to proceed with a pivotal global Phase 3 trial for hospital‑acquired and ventilator‑associated bacterial pneumonia. The confirmation allows the RIV‑TARGET study to recruit patients in the U.S. as part of the global program, which is on track to read out results by the end of 2027. BV100 is a novel intravenous formulation of an antibiotic. The drug already holds Qualified Infectious Disease Product (QIDP) designation from the FDA, making it eligible for priority review and other regulatory incentives. BioVersys aims to advance the trial across multiple regions in the coming months. AC Immune Initiates Phase 1 Clinical Trial The Lausanne-based clinical-stage biopharmaceutical company AC Immune develops precision therapeutics for neurodegenerative diseases, including Alzheimer’s and Parkinson’s disease. The company announced that the first participant has been dosed in a Phase 1 trial of its lead small molecule, ACI-19764, an orally available, brain-penetrant drug candidate that specifically inhibits the NLRP3 inflammasome. Targeting the NLRP3 inflammasome provides an opportunity to reduce the chronic inflammation thought to be associated with disease progression in multiple inflammatory disorders, metabolic diseases, and neurological diseases, including Alzheimer’s disease (AD), Parkinson’s disease (PD), amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) and frontotemporal dementia.The trial in healthy volunteers will evaluate safety, tolerability, and how the drug behaves in the body, with initial data expected in the second half of 2026. Santhera Demonstrates Long-Term Safety Santhera develops innovative medicines for rare neuromuscular diseases with high unmet medical need. The company announced that long-term real-world data from up to eight years of treatment with its dissociative steroid AGAMREE in boys with Duchenne muscular dystrophy (DMD) were presented at the 2026 Muscular Dystrophy Association Clinical & Scientific Conference. The analyses show that AGAMREE maintains effectiveness comparable to traditional corticosteroids, while offering safety advantages, including reduced risk of vertebral fractures, maintenance of normal growth, and fewer cataracts. The ongoing GUARDIAN study will continue to assess long-term outcomes such as muscle, bone, heart, respiratory, and eye health. Today AGAMREE is approved in multiple regions worldwide, including the U.S., EU, UK, Switzerland, China, Hong Kong, and Canada, and is being commercialized through regional partners.
